skill.name: voice-agent-expert
skill.description: Troubleshoot and operate a local LiveKit and Letta voice agent stack, including startup checks, token generation, and common failure fixes.
skill.summary: Debug local LiveKit and Letta voice agent setup issues with focused checks and operational guidance.
skill.author: EGAdams

security_audit.summary: The static scanner found many dangerous patterns; most Markdown backtick hits are false positives from documentation formatting. The executable scripts do read local environment files, use shell commands, generate and print a LiveKit bearer token, and start local processes, so publication should require maintainer review and path hardening. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

high_findings.title: Local Secret File Access and Token Exposure
high_findings.description: The helper scripts load API keys and secrets from hardcoded .env paths. The token generator uses the LiveKit API secret to create a JWT and prints that bearer token, which can expose room access if terminal output is logged or shared.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads local .env files and handles named API keys and secrets. It does not send them to an unknown endpoint, but printing a generated JWT is a clear exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Execution in Diagnostic Helpers
medium_findings.description: Diagnostic and startup helpers execute shell commands to inspect ports and processes. Current arguments appear fixed, but shell=True increases future injection risk if user-controlled values are added later.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls use shell=True, but the observed callers pass fixed ports and package names. This is a real hardening issue rather than confirmed command injection.

medium_findings.title: Operational Commands Can Kill Processes or Remove Lock Files
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends pkill commands and removal of temporary PID or lock files. These commands are intended for troubleshooting but can disrupt unrelated local services if patterns match broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly shown in troubleshooting instructions and include process termination and file removal. The intent is legitimate but operationally risky.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Personal Paths Reduce Safety and Portability
medium_findings.description: Scripts and docs reference absolute paths under a specific home directory. Running the skill outside that environment can fail, leak local project structure, or encourage unsafe path edits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The absolute paths are repeated across scripts and documentation. They are not malicious by themselves, but they create unsafe assumptions for marketplace users.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick findings are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced shell examples, not Ruby execution. They still document commands a user might run, but they are not executable code in those Markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documents and the backticks delimit prose or shell examples. No Ruby file or Ruby interpreter path is present in those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Local Services and Official Playground
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings reference localhost services and the LiveKit agents playground. No evidence found that the skill sends secrets to an unknown external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible local service checks or a documented LiveKit testing page. This lowers exfiltration concern, though users should still avoid sharing generated tokens.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and SAM Database Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and Windows SAM database hits do not match cryptographic code or SAM database access in the reviewed files. They appear to be keyword or heuristic collisions in prose and printed headings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe voice-agent modes or print status banners. No evidence found of hashing, encryption choices, or Windows credential database access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Environment Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts inspect .env files and named API key variables for LiveKit, Deepgram, and OpenAI. This is expected for the workflow but sensitive for a community marketplace skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variable names and .env paths are explicit. The behavior is semantically tied to setup, but it handles secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Process and Port Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts use shell pipelines for process and port discovery. This broad system inspection can expose process details and should be replaced with safer APIs where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct shell invocations. The current inputs are controlled, but shell-based inspection is still a risky pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Long-Running Local Service Startup
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents starting LiveKit and voice-agent processes, including background execution and exec replacement. This is legitimate for the skill but should not run without explicit user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process startup behavior is explicit and operationally necessary. It is not stealthy, but it changes local runtime state.

content.value_statement: Local voice-agent stacks fail in ways that are hard to diagnose quickly. This skill provides focused checks, recovery steps, and helper scripts for LiveKit and Letta workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains START mode versus DEV mode for local LiveKit agent testing.
Provides quick checks for Letta, LiveKit, and voice-agent processes.
Documents recovery steps for duplicate agents, stale rooms, and expired tokens.
Includes scripts to diagnose local ports, packages, environment variables, and services.
Includes a token generator for connecting to a LiveKit voice room.
Provides startup guidance for a local Letta and LiveKit voice-agent workflow.
limitations: Scripts contain hardcoded paths for the original author environment.
The workflow assumes local Letta, LiveKit, and Python dependencies are already installed.
Helper scripts read local .env files and should be reviewed before execution.
It is focused on local troubleshooting, not production deployment design.

use_cases.title: Recover a Local Voice Agent
use_cases.description: Diagnose duplicate processes, stale rooms, timeout errors, and missing services in a local LiveKit setup.
use_cases.target_user: Developer running Letta voice agents locally

use_cases.title: Prepare a Test Session
use_cases.description: Check required services, generate a room token, and connect through the LiveKit testing workflow.
use_cases.target_user: AI engineer testing voice interaction flows

use_cases.title: Document Operational Runbooks
use_cases.description: Convert known voice-agent failure modes into repeatable checks for team troubleshooting.
use_cases.target_user: Platform maintainer supporting local agent tooling

prompt_templates.title: Basic Health Check
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs a quick local status review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the voice-agent guidance to check whether Letta, LiveKit, and the voice-agent worker are ready for local testing.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Connection Timeout
prompt_templates.scenario: A local voice session fails before the agent joins.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose a LiveKit room connection timeout. Focus on agent mode, duplicate processes, stale rooms, and token age.

prompt_templates.title: Review Startup Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer wants to start services in the correct order.
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk through the safest startup sequence for a local Letta and LiveKit voice agent. Identify any environment or path assumptions first.

prompt_templates.title: Harden the Scripts
prompt_templates.scenario: A maintainer wants to adapt the helper scripts for a team.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the voice-agent helper scripts for portability and security. Suggest changes for configurable paths, safer subprocess usage, and token handling.

output_examples.input: My LiveKit voice room times out before the agent joins.
output_examples.output: The response would check whether the worker is in DEV mode, look for duplicate voice-agent processes, confirm LiveKit is listening, and recommend a clean restart if stale rooms are likely.

output_examples.input: I need a fresh token for a local voice test.
output_examples.output: The response would explain which environment variables are required, where the helper script expects them, and how to protect the generated token from logs or screenshots.

output_examples.input: Can I publish these scripts for my team?
output_examples.output: The response would highlight hardcoded paths, .env file access, shell command usage, and process-kill commands that should be changed before shared use.

best_practices: Review and replace hardcoded paths before running helper scripts.
Treat generated LiveKit tokens as sensitive bearer credentials.
Confirm process-kill commands match only the intended local services.
anti_patterns: Running the scripts before inspecting which .env files they read.
Sharing terminal output that contains generated connection tokens.
Using START mode for direct local voice-agent testing when DEV mode is required.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps troubleshoot a local LiveKit and Letta voice-agent setup, including service checks, duplicate agents, stale rooms, and tokens.

faq.question: Does it work for production deployments?
faq.answer: No. The content focuses on local testing and operational debugging, not production architecture or security controls.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The included scripts read .env files, handle API secrets, print a generated token, and execute local shell commands.

faq.question: Are the Markdown command examples executable by themselves?
faq.answer: No. The Markdown files document commands, but users or agents could still run those commands manually.

faq.question: What should be changed before team use?
faq.answer: Make paths configurable, reduce shell=True usage, avoid printing tokens unnecessarily, and add explicit confirmation before killing processes.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
